Had these channels not been there, large-scale flooding would have occurred. Wherever channels are ill-defined or choked, flooding is a common phenomenon. The flow of... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: DRAINAGE SYSTEM, 18 INDIA : PHYSICAL ENVIRONMENT, AFGHANISTAN, 19 DRAINAGE SYSTEM. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Drainage System is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that you have observed water flowing through the rivers, nalas and even channels during rainy season which drain the excess water.

The text explains that had these channels not been there, large-scale flooding would have occurred.

The text explains that wherever channels are ill-defined or choked, flooding is a common phenomenon.

The text explains that the flow of water through well-defined channels is known as ‘drainage’ and the network of such channels is called a ‘drainage system’.

The text explains that the drainage pattern of an area is the outcome of the geological time period, nature and structure of rocks, topography, slope, amount of water flowing and the periodicity of the flow.

The text explains that do you have a river near your village or city?

The text explains that have you ever been there for boating or bathing?

The text explains that is it perennial (always with water) or ephemeral (water during rainy season, and dry, otherwise)?

The text explains that do you know that rivers flow in the same direction?

The text explains that you have studied about slopes in the other two textbooks of geography (NCERT, 2024) in this class .

The text explains that can you, then, explain the reason for water flowing from one direction to the other?

The text explains that why do the rivers originating from the Himalayas in the northern India and the Western Ghat in the southern India flow towards the east and discharge their waters in the Bay of Bengal?

The text explains that a river drains the water collected from a specific area, which is called its ‘catchment area’.

The text explains that an area drained by a river and its tributaries DRAINAGE SYSTEM C H A P T E R Figure 3.1 : A River in the Mountainous Region Important Drainage Patterns (i) The drainage pattern resembling the branches of a tree is known as “dendritic” the examples of which are the rivers of northern plain.

The text explains that (ii) When the rivers originate from a hill and flow in all directions, the drainage pattern is known as ‘radial’.

The text explains that the rivers originating from the Amarkantak range present a good example of it.

The text explains that (iii) When the primary tributaries of rivers flow parallel to each other and secondary tributaries join them at right angles, the pattern is known as ‘trellis’.

The text explains that (iv) When the rivers discharge their waters from all directions in a lake or depression, the pattern is know as ‘centripetal’.

The text explains that find out some of the patterns in the topo sheet given in Chapter 5 of Practical Work in Geography– Part I (NCERT).

The text explains that 19 DRAINAGE SYSTEM is called a drainage basin.

The text explains that what happens in one part of the basin or watershed directly affects the other parts and the unit as a whole.

The text explains that indian drainage system may be divided on various bases.

The text explains that nearly 77 per cent of the drainage area consisting of the Ganga, the Brahmaputra, the Mahanadi, the Krishna, etc. is oriented towards the Bay of Bengal while 23 per cent comprising the Indus, the Narmada, the Tapi, the Mahi and the Periyar systems discharge their waters in the Arabian Sea.

The text explains that if you look at the Figure 3.2 you can see that many rivers have their sources in the Himalayas and discharge their waters either in the Bay of Bengal or in the Arabian Sea.

The text explains that dRAINAGE SYSTEMS OF INDIA Indian drainage system consists of a large number of small and big rivers.

The text explains that it is the outcome of the evolutionary process of the three major physiographic units and the nature and characteristics of precipitation.

The text explains that tHE HIMALAYAN DRAINAGE The Himalayan drainage system has evolved through a long geological history.

The text explains that since these are fed both by melting of snow and precipitation, rivers of this system are perennial.

The text explains that while entering the plains, they form depositional features like flat valleys, ox-bow lakes, flood plains, braided channels, and deltas near the river mouth.

The text explains that it is opined that in due course of time Indo– Brahma river was dismembered into three main drainage systems: (i) the Indus and its five tributaries in the western part; (ii) the Ganga and its Himalayan tributaries in the central part; and (iii) the stretch of the Brahmaputra in Assam and its Himalayan tributaries in the eastern part.

The text explains that the dismemberment was probably due to the Pleistocene upheaval in the western Himalayas, including the uplift of the Potwar Plateau (Delhi Ridge), which acted as the water divide between the Indus and Ganga drainage systems.

The text explains that likewise, the down- thrusting of the Malda gap area between the Rajmahal hills and the Meghalaya plateau during the mid-pleistocene period, diverted the Ganga and the Brahmaputra systems to flow towards the Bay of Bengal.

The text explains that tHE RIVER SYSTEMS OF THE HIMALAYAN DRAINAGE The Himalayan drainage consists of several river systems but the following are the major river systems: The Indus System It is one of the largest river basins of the world, covering an area of 11,65,000 sq. km (in India it is 321, 289 sq. km and a total length of 2,880 km (in India 1,114 km).

The text explains that after flowing in the northwest direction between the Ladakh and Zaskar ranges, it passes through Ladakh and Baltistan.

The text explains that it finally emerges out of the hills near Attock where it receives the Kabul river on its right bank.

The text explains that the other important tributaries joining the right bank of the Indus are the Khurram, the Tochi, the Gomal, the Viboa and the Sangar.

The text explains that 21 DRAINAGE SYSTEM discharges into the Arabian Sea, east of Karachi.

The text explains that it is a very important tributary as it feeds the canal system of the Bhakra Nangal project.

The text explains that the Ganga System The Ganga is the most important river of India both from the point of view of its basin and cultural significance.

The text explains that the Alaknanda has its source in the Satopanth glacier above Badrinath.

The text explains that the Ganga Do you Know? ‘Namami Gange Programme’, is an Integrated Conservation Mission, approved as “Flagship Programme” by the Union Government in June 2014 with the twin objectives of effective abatement of pollution, conservation and rejuvenation of the National River Ganga.

The text explains that main pillars of the Namami Gange Programme are: • Sewerage Treatment Infrastructure • River-Front Development • River-Surface Cleaning • Bio-Diversity • Afforestation • Public Awareness • Industrial Effluent Monitoring • Ganga Gram You may explore about this project at http://nmcg.nic.in/NamamiGanga.aspx# 2026-27

The text explains that 22 INDIA : PHYSICAL ENVIRONMENT river system is the largest in India having a number of perennial and non-perennial rivers originating in the Himalayas in the north and the Peninsula in the south, respectively.

The text explains that the Son is its major right bank t r i b u t a r y .

The text explains that the Yamuna, the western most and the longest tributary of the Ganga, has its source in the Yamunotri glacier on the western slopes of Banderpunch range (6,316 m).

The text explains that it is joined by the Chambal, the Sind, the Betwa and the Ken on its right bank which originates from the Peninsular plateau while the Hindan, the Rind, the Sengar, the Varuna, etc. join it on its left bank.

The text explains that (a) The Son (c) The Yamuna (b) The Narmada (d) The Luni (v) Which one of the following is the place of confluence of the Alaknanda and the Bhagirathi?

The text explains that (a) Vishnu Prayag (c) Karan Prayag (b) Rudra Prayag (d) Deva Prayag 2.

The text explains that (i) River Basin and Watershed (ii) Dendritic and Trellis drainage pattern (iii) Radial and Centripetal drainage pattern (iv) Delta and Estuary 3.

The text explains that (i) What are the socio-economic advantages of inter-linking of rivers in India?

The text explains that 26 INDIA : PHYSICAL ENVIRONMENT (ii) Write three characterstics of the Peninsular river.

The text explains that (i) What are the important characteristic features of north Indian rivers?

Source: NCERT Class 11 India Physical Environment, Chapter 3: Drainage System

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Drainage System, which statement is supported by the source text about DRAINAGE SYSTEMS OF INDIA?
options: 18 INDIA : PHYSICAL ENVIRONMENT Figure 3.2 : Major Rivers of India AFGHANISTAN 2026-27
The boundary line separating one drainage basin from the other is known as the watershed.
The catchments of large rivers are called river basins while those of small rivulets and rills are often referred to as watersheds.
There is, however, a slight difference between a river basin and a watershed.
